query: The Lucy Maud Montgomery novel about the character Anne Shirley was first translated into Japanese by what woman?When ready, output the final answer enclosed in <answer> and </answer> tags. Do not generate any content after the </answer> tag.
query_analysis:
Concise Summary: The query seeks the name of the woman who translated Anne Shirley's novel into Japanese, requiring factual verification of literary translation history.

Required Skills:
Literary knowledge, translation history research, fact verification

Relevant Tools:
Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool (for structured literary data), Ground_Google_Search_Tool (for cross-referencing specific translation credits)

Additional Considerations:
Ensure the answer distinguishes between the original author (Montgomery) and the translator; verify the specific Japanese translation edition as multiple may exist.

Muraoka received a copy of "Anne of Green Gables" in 1939 from her friend, a Canadian missionary named Loretta Leonard Shaw.[2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka), [4](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09555803.2014.900513) She undertook the translation during World War II, often bringing her draft with her during air raids.[2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka), [4](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09555803.2014.900513) Her work was later added to the Japanese school curriculum in the 1970s.[2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka) Hanako Muraoka is also known for her work as a novelist and for her radio show where she read news to children, earning her the affectionate title "Aunty Radio" (Rajio no Obasan).[2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka), [5](https://shopatsullivan.com/products/annes-cradle-the-life-works-of-hanako-muraoka-japanese-translator-of-anne-of-green-gables)']}}

result:
Hanako Muraoka was the first woman to translate Lucy Maud Montgomery's "Anne of Green Gables" into Japanese.[1](https://annemanuscript.ca/stories/anne-in-japan/), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka), [3](https://www.arc-japanese-translation.com/blog/20160118/index.html), [4](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09555803.2014.900513) Her translation, titled "Akage no An" (Red-haired Anne), was first published in 1952 and became a bestseller, significantly contributing to the book's enduring popularity in Japan.[1](https://annemanuscript.ca/stories/anne-in-japan/), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka), [4](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09555803.2014.900513), [5](https://shopatsullivan.com/products/annes-cradle-the-life-works-of-hanako-muraoka-japanese-translator-of-anne-of-green-gables)

Muraoka received a copy of "Anne of Green Gables" in 1939 from her friend, a Canadian missionary named Loretta Leonard Shaw.[2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka), [4](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09555803.2014.900513) She undertook the translation during World War II, often bringing her draft with her during air raids.[2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka), [4](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09555803.2014.900513) Her work was later added to the Japanese school curriculum in the 1970s.[2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka) Hanako Muraoka is also known for her work as a novelist and for her radio show where she read news to children, earning her the affectionate title "Aunty Radio" (Rajio no Obasan).[2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanako_Muraoka), [5](https://shopatsullivan.com/products/annes-cradle-the-life-works-of-hanako-muraoka-japanese-translator-of-anne-of-green-gables)

### Process Summary

1.  **Initial Search (Wikipedia_RAG):** The first action attempted to retrieve information regarding the Japanese translation of *Anne of Green Gables* using a structured search tool. However, this step returned a list of general pages about the book, the character, and related media (films, TV series) without explicitly identifying the specific translator's name in the abstracts provided.
2.  **Secondary Search (Ground_Google_Search):** To overcome the lack of specific data in the first step, a web search was conducted. This action successfully retrieved detailed information confirming that **Hanako Muraoka** was the first woman to translate the novel into Japanese. The search results provided specific context, noting that her translation, titled *Akage no An* (Red-haired Anne), was published in 1952 and became a bestseller.

### Answer

<answer>Hanako Muraoka</answer>
